title: SVM & k-NN Quiz
description: Test your knowledge on Hyperplanes, the Kernel Trick, and Euclidean distance.

questions.question: What is the primary geometric goal of a Support Vector Machine (SVM) when classifying linearly separable data?

options.text: To cluster the data into concentric rings.
To draw thousands of decision trees and average the slope.
To locate a 'Hyperplane' that maximizes the mathematical distance (margin) between the separating line and the nearest distinct classified data points.
To calculate the probability curve using Bayes Theorem.

questions.question: When data points CANNOT be separated by a straight line in lower dimensions, what famous mathematical concept do SVMs utilize to project the points into a higher dimension to find a solution?

options.text: The Softmax Function
Gradient Descent
Matrix Factorization
The Kernel Trick

questions.question: Why is the k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN) algorithm often referred to as a 'Lazy Learner' in Machine Learning data science?

options.text: Because it doesn't actually 'train' or calculate mathematical weights during a training phase; it simply memorizes the training data and performs calculations dynamically only when queried.
Because it refuses to run unless the data is small.
Because it only uses a tiny subset of the available data.
Because it is written exclusively in Python.

questions.question: If you are running a k-NN classification algorithm, what happens if you set the hyperparameter `k = 1`?

options.text: The model will violently overfit to noise, classifying any new dot identically to the single closest point regardless of surrounding clusters.
The model will perfectly generalize the data.
The model throws a DivisionByZero Error internally.
The algorithm essentially turns into Linear Regression.

questions.question: What happens if you set the 'k' hyperparameter in a k-NN model to equal the exact total number of points in your entire dataset?

options.text: The algorithm will predict randomly.
It will always perfectly classify the new point.
It will always just blindly predict whichever class label currently happens to be the absolute majority across the dataset for every single query.
It will execute an SQL JOIN query.
